questionText: In a binary min-heap stored in an array (1-indexed), the children of the node at index i are at:
explanation: For a 1-indexed array heap, node i's left child is at 2i, right child at 2i+1, and parent at ⌊i/2⌋. This implicit structure needs no pointers.
distractorRationales: i+1 and i+2 are adjacent array cells, not the heap's children.
i/2 is the parent index, not a child.
i−1 and i+1 are array neighbors, unrelated to the heap parent-child mapping.

questionText: The time complexity of extracting the minimum from a binary min-heap of n elements is:
explanation: Extract-min returns the root (O(1)) but must restore the heap by moving the last element to the root and sifting it down, which takes O(log n) for the heap's height.
distractorRationales: Reading the minimum is O(1), but removing it and restoring heap order is O(log n).
O(n) would be a linear scan, unnecessary given the heap structure.
O(n log n) is the cost of sorting all elements, not a single extraction.

questionText: Finding (peeking at) the maximum element in a max-heap takes:
explanation: In a max-heap the maximum is always at the root (index 1), so peeking at it is O(1). Removing it would cost O(log n).
distractorRationales: O(log n) is the cost of removing the max, not peeking at it.
O(n) would be needed in an unordered array, not a heap.
O(n log n) is a sorting cost, irrelevant to a single peek.

questionText: Inserting a new element into a binary heap of n elements takes:
options: O(log n) time, via sift-up from the new leaf
explanation: The element is placed at the next leaf position and sifted up, swapping with its parent until heap order is restored — at most the height of the tree, O(log n).
distractorRationales: Insertion is O(log n) worst case, not always O(1) (though it can be O(1) amortized for some heap variants).
O(n) would require shifting all elements, which the heap avoids.
O(n log n) is a full-sort cost, not a single insertion.

questionText: A complete binary tree with n nodes has height:
explanation: A complete binary tree is as balanced as possible, so its height is ⌊log₂ n⌋. This is why heap operations that traverse a root-to-leaf path are O(log n).
distractorRationales: n/2 is roughly the number of leaves, not the height.
√n is not the height of a complete binary tree.
n−1 would be the height of a degenerate (path-like) tree, not a complete one.

questionText: Which operation is NOT efficiently supported (better than O(n)) by a standard binary heap?
options: Searching for an arbitrary element by value
explanation: A binary heap only orders along root-to-leaf paths, not globally, so finding an arbitrary value requires scanning up to all n elements — O(n). Extract-min, insert (O(log n)), and peek (O(1)) are all efficient.
distractorRationales: Extract-min is O(log n), efficient.
Insert is O(log n), efficient.
Peek-min is O(1), the most efficient operation.

questionText: A priority queue is most appropriately implemented with a binary heap rather than a sorted array when:
options: Insertions and extract-min are both frequent (heap gives O(log n) each vs. O(n) insertion in a sorted array)
Only peeking at the minimum is ever needed
Random indexed access is the main operation
explanation: A sorted array gives O(1) min but O(n) insertion (shifting). A heap balances both insert and extract-min at O(log n), making it ideal when both operations occur frequently.
distractorRationales: If only peeking is needed, even a single tracked variable suffices; a heap is overkill.
Static data could just be sorted once; the heap's advantage is handling dynamic updates.
Heaps do not provide efficient random indexed access; arrays do.

questionText: Building a heap from n arbitrary elements using the bottom-up 'heapify' (Floyd's build-heap) procedure takes:
explanation: Although each sift-down is O(log n), most nodes are near the leaves with small subtree heights. Summing height-weighted costs gives a geometric series bounded by O(n) — strictly better than n successive inserts (O(n log n)).
distractorRationales: O(n log n) is the cost of n individual insertions, which the bottom-up build-heap improves upon.
O(log n) is the height, not the cost of processing all n nodes.
O(n²) vastly overstates the build-heap cost.

questionText: In a binary min-heap with n nodes, the maximum element is guaranteed to be:
options: At one of the leaf nodes (in the last ⌈n/2⌉ positions)
explanation: In a min-heap every node is ≤ its descendants, so the maximum cannot have any children — it must be a leaf. Leaves occupy roughly the second half of the array (indices ⌊n/2⌋+1 .. n).
distractorRationales: The root holds the MINIMUM in a min-heap, not the maximum.
The maximum need not be at the second level; it must be a leaf.
Index 2 is an internal node for larger heaps, so it is not guaranteed to hold the max.

questionText: A d-ary heap (each node has up to d children) has height Θ(log_d n). For extract-min, the sift-down cost is:
options: O(d · log_d n), because each level compares among up to d children
explanation: A d-ary heap is shallower (height log_d n), but each sift-down level must find the smallest of up to d children (O(d) comparisons). Total sift-down cost is O(d · log_d n).
distractorRationales: The base of the log depends on d; it is log_d n, not log_2 n, and each level costs O(d).
O(d·n) overstates; sift-down follows one root-to-leaf path, not all nodes.
O(n/d) does not reflect the logarithmic height of the heap.

questionText: A Fibonacci heap improves Dijkstra's algorithm because it supports decrease-key in:
explanation: Fibonacci heaps offer O(1) amortized decrease-key and insert, with O(log n) amortized extract-min. This brings Dijkstra to O(E + V log V), better than the binary-heap O((V+E) log V) for dense graphs.
distractorRationales: O(log n) decrease-key is the binary-heap bound; Fibonacci heaps achieve O(1) amortized.
O(n) is far worse than what Fibonacci heaps provide.
O(log² n) is not the Fibonacci-heap decrease-key bound.

questionText: Heap sort uses a heap to sort n elements. Its overall time complexity is:
explanation: Heap sort builds a heap in O(n), then performs n extract-max operations, each O(log n), totaling O(n log n) in the worst case, with O(1) extra space.
distractorRationales: O(n) is only the build-heap phase; the n extractions add the log factor.
O(n²) is the cost of simple sorts like bubble sort, not heap sort.
O(log n) is the cost of a single operation, not sorting all n elements.

questionText: In an array-based binary heap (1-indexed), the parent of the node at index i is at index:
explanation: For a 1-indexed array heap, the parent of node i is at ⌊i/2⌋, while children are at 2i and 2i+1.
distractorRationales: 2i is the left child, not the parent.
2i+1 is the right child, not the parent.
i-1 is the previous array slot, unrelated to the heap parent.

questionText: A max-heap is a complete binary tree in which:
options: Every parent is greater than or equal to its children
Every parent is less than its children
The tree is a binary search tree
explanation: The max-heap property requires each node to be ≥ its children, so the maximum is at the root. (A min-heap is the opposite.)
distractorRationales: Parent less than children is the min-heap property.
A heap is NOT a binary search tree; it has no left/right ordering across subtrees.
In a max-heap, the largest value is at the root, not in the leaves.

questionText: Decreasing the key of an element in a binary min-heap requires:
options: Sift-up (bubble the element toward the root), O(log n)
explanation: Decreasing a key in a min-heap may violate the heap property with its parent, so the element is sifted UP (swapped with smaller-than-it parents) until order is restored — O(log n).
distractorRationales: Sift-down is for increasing a key (or extract-min restoration), not decreasing it in a min-heap.
A full rebuild is unnecessary; a single sift-up suffices.
A decreased key can violate the heap property, so action is required.

questionText: Which abstract operation does a priority queue NOT typically provide in O(log n) or better?
explanation: Priority queues optimize access to the extreme (min or max) element. Searching for an arbitrary element is O(n) because the heap is not globally ordered. Insert is O(log n), extract is O(log n), and peek is O(1).
distractorRationales: Insert is O(log n) via sift-up.
Extract-min/max is O(log n) via sift-down.
Peek at the extreme is O(1).

questionText: Heapifying the array [4, 10, 3, 5, 1] into a MAX-heap (using bottom-up build-heap) yields which root?
explanation: Build-heap sifts internal nodes down. The maximum value, 10, ends up at the root of a max-heap regardless of the procedure used.
distractorRationales: 4 is the original root, but build-heap moves the largest (10) up.
5 is not the maximum, so it cannot be the max-heap root.
1 is the minimum, the farthest from the root in a max-heap.

questionText: A binomial heap supports merging (union) of two heaps with n total elements in:
explanation: A binomial heap is a collection of binomial trees; merging two of them is like binary addition of their tree lists, taking O(log n). This is faster than the O(n) merge of two binary heaps.
distractorRationales: O(1) worst-case merge is a Fibonacci-heap property (amortized), not a binomial heap's worst case.
O(n) is the cost of merging two simple binary heaps, which binomial heaps improve upon.
O(n log n) overstates the binomial-heap union cost.

questionText: Why is a heap preferred over a sorted array for implementing a priority queue with frequent insertions?
options: A heap inserts in O(log n), whereas keeping a sorted array ordered costs O(n) per insertion
A heap is always smaller in memory
A sorted array cannot store priorities
explanation: Inserting into a sorted array requires shifting elements (O(n)), while a heap inserts in O(log n) via sift-up and still gives O(1) access to the min/max — better for insertion-heavy workloads.
distractorRationales: Heaps do not give O(1) search for arbitrary elements; that is not the advantage.
Memory footprint is similar; the advantage is insertion time.
A sorted array can store priorities; the issue is its O(n) insertion cost.

questionText: The k-th smallest element of an array can be found using a max-heap of size k in time:
explanation: Maintain a max-heap of the k smallest seen so far: for each of n elements, push and (if size > k) pop the max — each O(log k). Total O(n log k), better than fully sorting when k ≪ n.
distractorRationales: O(n log n) is full sorting; the size-k heap does better when k is small.
O(k log n) does not account for processing all n elements.
O(n + k) is not achievable for comparison-based k-th selection via this heap method.
